title: OpenTelemetry Collector Pipeline Throughput and Backpressure

premiumApps.name: Splunk ITSI
premiumApps.note: optional — entity KPI overlays when collector_id maps to service entities

dataSources: `index=otel_metrics` and related metrics indexes with OpenTelemetry Collector internal telemetry scraped or remote-written into mstats-compatible metric_name families (`otelcol_receiver_accepted_*`, `otelcol_receiver_refused_*`, `otelcol_exporter_queue_size`, `otelcol_exporter_queue_capacity`, `otelcol_exporter_send_failed_*`, `otelcol_processor_dropped_*`, `otelcol_processor_batches_sent`, `otelcol_processor_memorylimiter_drops`, `otelcol_processor_batch_*`); `index=prometheus_metrics` (or `metrics`) with `container_cpu_usage_seconds_total` and `container_memory_working_set_bytes` for collector pods; `index` in (`otel`,`observability_logs`,`containers`) with `sourcetype=otel:collector` for exporter failures and queue language; lookup `otel_collector_inventory.csv` keyed on `collector_id`; `index=cmdb` with `sourcetype=otel_pipeline_inventory` for routing metadata joined on `collector_id`.
app: Splunk Distribution of OpenTelemetry Collector (Splunk Observability) + opentelemetry-collector-contrib builds + Splunk Connect for Kubernetes
description: Detects end-to-end OpenTelemetry Collector pipeline saturation and backpressure across logs, metrics, and traces by correlating internal receiver, processor, and exporter telemetry with collector logs and Kubernetes pod resource pressure, enriched with inventory and configuration management metadata for gateway and agent deployments shipping to Splunk HEC, OTLP, SignalFx, or Observability Cloud.
value: Platform and observability leaders see collector saturation before customers notice missing traces or delayed dashboards: rising exporter queues, refused receivers, memory limiter drops, and retry exhaustion are early warnings that ingest tokens, batch sizing, autoscaling, or downstream Splunk capacity need attention. Reliability governance gains a defensible narrative that separates intentional load shedding from silent data loss during migrations involving Splunk distributions and upstream contrib collectors.
grandmaExplanation: We watch factory belts that package measurements into boxes, move them through labeling stations, and load trucks to a warehouse. When a truck door rejects loads or a sorter jams, belts back up, sensors refuse new boxes, and foremen throttle intake so the hall does not overflow. We catch those jams early so crews open lanes, resize batches, or add gateways before anything gets quietly thrown away.
implementation: Enable collector internal telemetry into `otel_metrics`, forward `sourcetype=otel:collector` logs, scrape cAdvisor-style pod metrics into `prometheus_metrics`, maintain `otel_collector_inventory.csv` and `otel_pipeline_inventory` in cmdb, save `observability_uc_13_3_15_otel_pipeline_backpressure` on a five-minute cadence with `earliest=-2h@h`, route critical and high severities to platform observability on-call, and archive weekly CSV exports to a restricted evidence index.
detailedImplementation:
Step 1 — Prerequisites

Head of Platform owns this control jointly with observability engineers who operate OpenTelemetry Collector fleets in agent mode on Kubernetes nodes, gateway mode behind ingress controllers, and hybrid virtual-machine footprints, plus the teams that curate Splunk HTTP Event Collector tokens, Splunk Observability Cloud ingest tokens, SignalFx exporter endpoints, and OTLP receivers that terminate application traffic. This use case is deliberately about intra-collector saturation: receivers accept telemetry, processors reshape and batch it, exporters serialize and ship it, and internal queues translate momentary skew into refused items, dropped spans, or silent delay. UC-13.3.2 remains the home for Prometheus scrape and federation integrity into Splunk metrics indexes; UC-13.3.5 remains the home for alert-storm economics across paging vendors. UC-13.3.15 answers whether your collectors are running out of runway before telemetry reaches Splunk, Observability Cloud, or other sinks, using collector-native internal metrics, high-signal log lines, and Kubernetes pod pressure for the same pods that host collectors.

OpenTelemetry Collector pipelines are declared YAML: receivers ingest OTLP over gRPC or HTTP, processors mutate and gate traffic, exporters fan out to Splunk HEC, OTLP, SignalFx, Kafka, and other backends. The Splunk Distribution of OpenTelemetry Collector packages tested combinations of receivers, processors, and exporters with Splunk-forward defaults, while upstream OpenTelemetry Collector and opentelemetry-collector-contrib expose the same extension points with different build tags. Gateway collectors centralize tail sampling, authorization, and token hygiene; agent collectors co-locate with workloads and must survive node CPU and memory contention without starving application pods. Both modes expose Prometheus-style internal telemetry when you enable the telemetry service, typically scraped into metrics indexes or forwarded through a second collector that is not part of the pipeline under test.

Processor ordering is not cosmetic. The batch processor should usually sit after sampling and attribute enrichment that shrink cardinality, but before exporters that perform network writes. The memory limiter processor belongs early, often immediately after receivers, so it can refuse or drop before expensive transformations allocate large buffers. Anti-patterns include placing aggressive transform or regex-heavy processors ahead of batching on hot paths, which increases per-item CPU and widens flush latency, or placing memory limiting after batching, which defeats the goal of bounding heap before batch structures grow. Document your declared order per environment and compare it to vendor guidance whenever you uplift images.

Exporter retry and sending queue settings determine how backpressure surfaces. OTLP exporters support gRPC and HTTP transports; gRPC benefits from HTTP/2 multiplexing but exposes different keepalive and message size failure modes than HTTP/protobuf. Splunk HEC exporters surface HTTP status semantics: 401 and 403 imply token or capability misconfiguration, 413 implies payloads exceeding server limits or intermediate proxy ceilings, and 5xx implies receiver saturation or dependency outages. Configure sending_queue with explicit queue_size and enable persistent queue files when disk is available and security approves on-disk buffering, understanding that persistent_storage trades durability for operational complexity when disks fill or permissions break. Review queue consumers when Observability Cloud ingest tokens rotate; overlap old and new tokens during rotation windows to avoid retry storms that masquerade as application outages.

Kubernetes DaemonSet agents should carry resource requests and limits aligned with expected spans-per-second and log megabytes-per-minute, with vertical pod autoscaler or cluster autoscaler policies that do not lag so far behind traffic ramps that memory_limiter drops become your first signal. Gateway Deployments behind horizontal pod autoscalers need enough replicas that exporter concurrency matches downstream Splunk indexer or cloud ingest capacity. The k8sattributes processor enriches telemetry with cluster, namespace, and pod metadata; misconfiguration can explode label cardinality, which indirectly saturates exporters and indexer pipelines even when raw byte rates look stable.

RBAC and secret hygiene are mandatory. Read-only automation accounts should scrape internal metrics endpoints or query metrics indexes without cluster-admin kubeconfigs. Store Splunk HEC and Observability Cloud tokens in vault-backed secrets, rotate with overlap, and never paste raw tokens into saved searches. Network policy must allow OTLP gRPC from application namespaces to gateway collectors, and from collectors to Splunk endpoints, without flattening all segmentation. Legal reviewers should confirm trace and log samples forwarded for diagnostics respect data minimization policies.

Differentiation recap: UC-13.3.15 focuses on OpenTelemetry Collector pipeline throughput, exporter queue occupancy, processor drops, memory limiter drops, batch flush behavior, and correlated pod resource pressure, not Prometheus target scrape health in isolation, not Alertmanager paging storms, and not pure Splunk indexer replication narratives.

Step 2 — Configure data collection

Lane A lands OpenTelemetry Collector internal metrics into a metrics index such as otel_metrics using mstats-compatible metric names. Enable the collector telemetry section so Prometheus exposition or OTLP self-telemetry exports counters and gauges including otelcol_receiver_accepted_spans, otelcol_receiver_refused_spans, otelcol_exporter_queue_size, otelcol_exporter_queue_capacity, otelcol_exporter_send_failed_spans, otelcol_processor_dropped_spans, otelcol_processor_batches_sent, otelcol_processor_batch_batch_send_size, and otelcol_processor_memorylimiter_drops. Preserve service_instance_id, exporter, and receiver labels when present so per-pipeline analytics remain meaningful. If your deployment renames internal metrics, align FIELDALIAS rules before this search runs in production.

Lane B captures Splunk Distribution and contrib collector logs with sourcetype=otel:collector into observability or containers indexes. Structured JSON logging simplifies extraction of exporter names, retry counts, and gRPC status messages. Tune TRUNCATE for long stack traces. Ensure gateway and agent pods share the same sourcetype naming so multisearch arms do not miss gateway-only failures.

Lane C scrapes kubelet or Prometheus cAdvisor style series such as container_cpu_usage_seconds_total and container_memory_working_set_bytes for pods whose names match otel, splunk-otel, collector, or opentelemetry substrings. These series often live in prometheus_metrics or metrics indexes populated by Prometheus servers or OpenTelemetry Collector prometheus receivers. Align pod labels with otel_collector_inventory.csv collector_id keys when possible.

Lane D maintains otel_collector_inventory.csv with columns collector_id, env, owner_team, role, image_tag, optional gateway_agent flag, and optional notes about OTLP gRPC versus HTTP exposure. Refresh weekly from GitOps repositories or image inventories. Lane E maintains cmdb sourcetype=otel_pipeline_inventory with one row per collector_id after deduplication, carrying routing metadata such as primary exporter family, HEC URL host, Observability Cloud realm, or Kafka topic names. The saved search joins both for human-readable routing without embedding secrets.

props.conf should normalize metric_name fields for mstats rows, preserve service_instance_id, and map host and pod fields from Kubernetes metadata extensions when collectors run as sidecars. For HEC forwards, confirm indexes and sourcetypes applied at ingest do not strip internal diagnostic labels.

Example curl helpers for design reviews from bastion hosts with credentials:

```bash
kubectl -n observability get pods -l app.kubernetes.io/name=splunk-otel-collector -o wide
kubectl -n observability logs deploy/otel-collector-gateway --tail=200
curl -fsS http://otel-collector.observability.svc.cluster.local:8888/metrics | rg otelcol_exporter_queue
```

Pre-save validation: otel_metrics must return non-zero internal metrics during steady traffic; otel:collector must show benign startup lines before error filters engage; prometheus_metrics must show pod series for collector DaemonSets in the same window.

Step 3 — Create the search and alert

Save the SPL as observability_uc_13_3_15_otel_pipeline_backpressure with a five-minute schedule during steady production, dispatch.earliest_time=-2h@h, dispatch.latest_time=now, and alert when severity is critical or high. Throttle duplicate collector_id and pipeline_type pairs for twenty minutes unless severity escalates. Include recommended_action, queue_pct_full, and exporter_failed_rate in pager narratives so incident commanders open exporter runbooks before muting.

Pipeline narrative: multisearch fans internal metrics arms for receivers and exporters, internal metrics arms for memory limiter and batch sizing, collector log lanes, and Kubernetes pod resource lanes so a silent metrics lane cannot hide log-proven exporter failures. streamstats computes five-minute sliding deltas for accepted, refused, and failed counters per collector_id and pipeline_type. eventstats adds median accepted-rate context per collector_id for baseline hints. case encodes criticality when queues stay above eighty-five percent for ten minutes, exporter failure ratios exceed five percent, or memory limiter drops remain positive. coalesce backfills pod_name and env fields after inventory joins. The first join attaches otel_collector_inventory.csv ownership; the second join attaches cmdb otel_pipeline_inventory routing metadata using the exact subsearch form required for auditability.

Fenced SPL must match the spl JSON field exactly:

```spl
`comment("UC-13.3.15 OTel Collector pipeline backpressure multisearch internal metrics logs kube cmdb enrichment earliest=-2h@h")`
| multisearch
    [ | mstats latest(_value) AS mv WHERE index=otel_metrics metric_name IN ("otelcol_receiver_accepted_spans","otelcol_receiver_accepted_metric_points","otelcol_receiver_accepted_log_records","otelcol_receiver_refused_spans","otelcol_receiver_refused_metric_points","otelcol_receiver_refused_log_records") span=1m BY metric_name service_instance_id exporter receiver
      | eval collector_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(service_instance_id,"otel_unk"))))
      | eval pipeline_type=case(match(metric_name,"log_record"),"logs", match(metric_name,"metric_point"),"metrics", match(metric_name,"span"),"traces", true(),"mixed")
      | eval lane="mstats_rx"
      | fields _time collector_id pipeline_type metric_name mv exporter receiver lane ]
    [ | mstats latest(_value) AS mv WHERE index=otel_metrics metric_name IN ("otelcol_exporter_queue_size","otelcol_exporter_queue_capacity","otelcol_exporter_send_failed_spans","otelcol_exporter_send_failed_metric_points","otelcol_exporter_send_failed_log_records","otelcol_processor_dropped_spans","otelcol_processor_batches_sent") span=1m BY metric_name service_instance_id exporter
      | eval collector_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(service_instance_id,"otel_unk"))))
      | eval pipeline_type=case(match(metric_name,"log_record"),"logs", match(metric_name,"metric_point"),"metrics", match(metric_name,"span"),"traces", match(metric_name,"processor_batches"),"batch", true(),"mixed")
      | eval lane="mstats_ex"
      | fields _time collector_id pipeline_type metric_name mv exporter lane ]
    [ | mstats latest(_value) AS mv WHERE index=otel_metrics metric_name IN ("otelcol_processor_memorylimiter_drops","otelcol_processor_batch_batch_send_size","otelcol_processor_batch_timeout_trigger_send") span=1m BY metric_name service_instance_id
      | eval collector_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(service_instance_id,"otel_unk"))))
      | eval pipeline_type=case(match(metric_name,"memorylimiter"),"memory", true(),"batch")
      | eval lane="mstats_lim"
      | fields _time collector_id pipeline_type metric_name mv lane ]
    [ search (index=otel OR index=observability_logs OR index=containers) sourcetype=otel:collector earliest=-2h@h latest=now
      | eval rawl=lower(_raw)
      | where match(rawl,"queue is full|exporter failure|resource exhausted|backpressure|memory.?limit|retry|payload too large|send failed|refused")
      | eval collector_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(collector_id,service_instance_id,pod,host,"otel_log"))))
      | eval pod_name=trim(toString(coalesce(pod,pod_name,"")))
      | eval pipeline_type="mixed"
      | eval metric_name="log_line_backpressure"
      | eval mv=1.0
      | eval lane="otel_logs"
      | fields _time collector_id pipeline_type metric_name mv lane pod_name ]
    [ | mstats latest(_value) AS mv WHERE index=prometheus_metrics metric_name IN ("container_cpu_usage_seconds_total","container_memory_working_set_bytes") span=1m BY pod namespace
      | where match(lower(pod),"otel|splunk-otel|collector|opentelemetry")
      | eval collector_id=lower(trim(toString(pod)))
      | eval pod_name=collector_id
      | eval pipeline_type="kube"
      | eval metric_name=metric_name
      | eval lane="kube_pod"
      | fields _time collector_id pipeline_type metric_name mv lane pod_name ]
| eval pod_name=coalesce(pod_name,"unknown_pod")
| eval pipeline_type=coalesce(pipeline_type,"mixed")
| bucket _time span=1m
| eval v_acc=if(match(metric_name,"receiver_accepted"),mv,0)
| eval v_ref=if(match(metric_name,"receiver_refused"),mv,0)
| eval v_drop=if(match(metric_name,"processor_dropped"),mv,0)
| eval v_fail=if(match(metric_name,"send_failed"),mv,0)
| eval v_qs=if(match(metric_name,"exporter_queue_size"),mv,null())
| eval v_qc=if(match(metric_name,"exporter_queue_capacity"),mv,null())
| eval v_mem=if(match(metric_name,"memorylimiter_drops"),mv,0)
| eval v_batch=if(match(metric_name,"batch_batch_send_size"),mv,null())
| stats sum(v_acc) AS acc sum(v_ref) AS ref sum(v_drop) AS drop sum(v_fail) AS fail max(v_qs) AS qsz max(v_qc) AS qcap sum(v_mem) AS memdrop perc95(v_batch) AS batch_p95_ms values(lane) AS lanes first(pod_name) AS pod_name BY _time collector_id pipeline_type
| sort 0 collector_id pipeline_type - _time
| streamstats window=5 global=f current=t first(acc) AS acc0 last(acc) AS acc1 BY collector_id pipeline_type
| streamstats window=5 global=f current=t first(ref) AS ref0 last(ref) AS ref1 BY collector_id pipeline_type
| streamstats window=5 global=f current=t first(fail) AS fail0 last(fail) AS fail1 BY collector_id pipeline_type
| eval receiver_accepted_rate=round((acc1-acc0)/5,4)
| eval receiver_refused_rate=round((ref1-ref0)/5,4)
| eval processor_dropped_rate=round(drop,4)
| eval attempts=receiver_accepted_rate+receiver_refused_rate+0.0001
| eval exporter_failed_rate=round(100*(fail1-fail0)/attempts,3)
| eval queue_pct_full=if(qcap>0 AND isnotnull(qsz), round(100*qsz/qcap,2), 0)
| streamstats window=10 global=f min(queue_pct_full) AS qmin10 BY collector_id pipeline_type
| eval memlimiter_drop_rate=round(memdrop,4)
| eval sustained_hi_queue=if(qmin10>85,1,0)
| eventstats median(receiver_accepted_rate) AS env_med_rx BY collector_id
| eval severity=case(sustained_hi_queue==1 OR exporter_failed_rate>5 OR memlimiter_drop_rate>0,"critical", queue_pct_full>70 OR exporter_failed_rate>2 OR receiver_refused_rate>0.25,"high", queue_pct_full>50 OR receiver_refused_rate>0.05 OR processor_dropped_rate>0,"medium", true(),"low")
| eval recommended_action=case(severity=="critical","scale_gateway_or_agents_tune_batch_memory_limiter_validate_hec_otlp_tokens_persistent_queue_disk", severity=="high","widen_exporter_sending_queue_review_retry_backoff_and_downstream_ingest_capacity", severity=="medium","inspect_processor_order_k8sattributes_resource_limits_and_tls_for_exporters", true(),"monitor_baseline_revisit_thresholds_after_change_window")
| join type=left max=0 collector_id
    [| inputlookup otel_collector_inventory.csv
     | fields collector_id env owner_team role image_tag ]
| join type=left collector_id [search index=cmdb sourcetype=otel_pipeline_inventory | dedup collector_id]
| eval env=coalesce(env,"unknown_env")
| eval owner_team=coalesce(owner_team,"unowned")
| table _time collector_id env owner_team pod_name pipeline_type receiver_accepted_rate receiver_refused_rate processor_dropped_rate exporter_failed_rate queue_pct_full batch_p95_ms memlimiter_drop_rate severity recommended_action
```

savedsearches.conf sketch:

```ini
[observability_uc_13_3_15_otel_pipeline_backpressure_alert]
cron_schedule = */5 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -2h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
enableSched = 1
action.email = 1
action.email.to = platform-observability-oncall@example.com
action.email.subject = OTel pipeline $result.severity$ $result.collector_id$ q=$result.queue_pct_full$
counttype = number of events
relation = greater than
quantity = 0
search = | savedsearch observability_uc_13_3_15_otel_pipeline_backpressure | where severity IN ("critical","high")
```

Performance: if Job Inspector shows multisearch queue time above internal service level objectives, summarize each arm into a five-minute summary index keyed on collector_id and pipeline_type, widen alert searches to earliest=-90m on summaries, and retain this full search for investigations. For very large internal metric cardinalities, drop unused receiver labels at scrape time and enforce allowlists on custom attributes before indexing.

Step 4 — Validate

Ground truth begins on a representative collector pod. curl the internal metrics endpoint and compare otelcol_exporter_queue_size and otelcol_exporter_queue_capacity to Splunk rows for the same service_instance_id within one collection interval. When numbers disagree, verify scrape targets, duplicate pod names across clusters, and delayed mstats ingestion before blaming parsers.

Deep dive with kubectl logs for the same pod and confirm exporter failure lines align with otel:collector events carrying queue is full or send failed language. For gateway collectors, compare gateway logs to agent logs to ensure backpressure is not misread as application failure.

OTLP transport validation: reproduce a controlled payload too large condition in non-production by exceeding configured max message sizes and confirm logs mention frame size or resource exhausted strings while internal metrics show refused counters moving.

HEC validation: intentionally revoke a lab token and observe 401 or 403 signatures in exporter logs while exporter_failed_rate rises in Splunk; restore tokens and confirm severities decay within two windows.

Synthetic validation: in lab, throttle downstream Observability Cloud ingest with vendor-approved tools, confirm queue_pct_full rises and critical severity appears, then remove throttle and confirm return to low.

Negative test: after healthy traffic with quiet logs and stable queues, filtering severity to critical and high should return zero rows for thirty consecutive runs.

Runbook linkage: document expected saturation during deliberate gateway failover drills so validators do not file defects when queues spike under approved change metadata.

Step 5 — Operationalize & Troubleshoot

Case 1 — Gateway exporter queue saturated during Observability Cloud ingest outage: scale gateway replicas, pause non-essential sampling, validate token health, and shift traffic to an alternate realm only under vendor guidance.

Case 2 — memory_limiter drops spike while autoscaler lags a traffic ramp: raise sane limits after profiling, increase replica minimums, and tune limiter thresholds to refuse earlier with clearer logs.

Case 3 — batchprocessor flush appears stuck after schema mismatch: fix resource attribute contracts, align semantic conventions, and verify transform processors are not multiplying spans before batching.

Case 4 — persistent storage for sending_queue fills the volume: expand disks, prune orphaned files under procedure, and validate file permissions after image upgrades.

Case 5 — gRPC keepalive misconfiguration triggers intermittent resets: align keepalive ping settings between clients and gateway, inspect service mesh idle timeouts, and compare with HTTP/protobuf fallback tests.

Case 6 — Splunk Cloud certificate expiry on HEC endpoints: renew ingress certificates, update trust stores on collectors, and validate SNI hostnames through corporate proxies.

Case 7 — Kafka exporter rebalance loop stalls exports: stabilize broker clusters, tune producer batching, and reduce collector concurrency only after broker health returns.

Case 8 — Kubernetes OOMKill on agent DaemonSets: increase memory requests, reduce max batch size, and ensure memory_limiter thresholds precede kernel kills.

Case 9 — sidecar regex parsing slowdown backs up receivers: relocate heavy parsing behind sampling, simplify expressions, or move parsing to a dedicated gateway tier.

Case 10 — processor pipeline order anti-pattern with transform after batch: reorder YAML under change control, replay canary traffic, and confirm batch_send_size distributions normalize.

Case 11 — upgrade-time deprecation warnings for receivers or processors: pin temporary compatibility configs, schedule config migrations, and validate internal metrics during rolling upgrades.

Case 12 — OTLP payload too large through corporate proxies: reduce per-batch item counts, enable compression, and raise proxy body limits only with security approval.

Closing checklist: five em-dash step headers are present; Step 3 fenced SPL matches the spl field exactly; multisearch lists five arms; coalesce, streamstats, eventstats, case, multisearch, inputlookup, and join appear; closing table includes the fourteen operational columns required for routing.
Operational notes expand on SignalFx exporter queues versus native OTLP exporters: SignalFx exporters may map internal queues differently than otellp exporters, yet otelcol_exporter_queue_size remains comparable across builds when the same telemetry stanza is enabled. Splunk Observability Cloud ingest typically expects authenticated requests with realm-scoped tokens; mismatched realm or org tokens produce 401 storms that show up as send_failed counters long before dashboards degrade. Upstream contrib builds may rename experimental metrics; maintain a translation table in props.conf when you mix splunk-otel-collector images with community images during migrations.

Gateway collectors should log exporter names on errors so Splunk analysts can pivot from metric spikes to exact exporter blocks. Agent collectors on Windows and Linux virtual machines follow the same internal metrics semantics when telemetry is enabled, but scrape networking differs: systemd-resolved, corporate proxies, and outbound allowlists frequently block OTLP gRPC while allowing HTTPS. Document those asymmetries in otel_pipeline_inventory rows to avoid false innocence during investigations.

When you route traces through tail sampling processors, refused spans at receivers may increase if applications burst unsampled traffic into collectors that enforce sampling at ingress. Distinguish intentional refusal from exporter failure using combination of otelcol_receiver_refused series and log lines. For logs pipelines, watch otelcol_receiver_accepted_log_records against exporter send failures; diverging trends imply queue growth. For metrics pipelines, correlate otelcol_receiver_accepted_metric_points with otelcol_exporter_send_failed_metric_points.

Teaching moment for junior engineers: backpressure is the system telling you it would rather shed load than lie about delivery guarantees. Your job is to decide whether shedding is acceptable, whether queues should grow, or whether horizontal scale is required. This search encodes conservative defaults; tune thresholds per cluster tier after observing seasonal traffic.

Audit evidence should include weekly CSV exports of the saved search filtered to critical and high severities, stored in a restricted index, with change ticket references when thresholds move. Executive summaries can chart rolling thirty-day counts of sustained_hi_queue without exposing raw customer payloads.

Finally, pair this control with capacity reviews: queue occupancy trends that creep upward quarter over quarter predict license and ingest tier upgrades more honestly than single-point-in-time estimates. Finance partners appreciate graphs that tie collector saturation to dollars before invoices arrive.

Capacity review cadence should include reviewing env_med_rx baselines exported from the same metrics indexes, comparing peak minutes to provisioned gateway CPU, and validating that new service onboarding estimates include OTLP concurrent streams. Database and mainframe bridge collectors deserve the same treatment when they proxy through Linux gateways.

Runbook cross-links: Splunk Observability Cloud status pages, Splunk Cloud maintenance calendars, Kafka cluster health dashboards, and corporate PKI renewal trackers belong in the same portal page as this saved search description so humans do not hunt bookmarks during incidents.

Training drills: twice per year, run tabletop exercises where engineers interpret multisearch outputs when two lanes disagree, ensuring on-call staff can explain why logs might show failures while metrics look quiet due to scrape loss.

Documentation hygiene: when renaming collector_id keys in inventory files, backfill historical summaries or accept brief breaks in longitudinal charts rather than silently mapping old IDs to new teams.

Governance: require peer review for YAML changes that touch exporter retry settings, because overly aggressive infinite retry configurations can amplify outages into stampedes.

Security: treat internal metrics scrape endpoints as sensitive; they reveal service graph topology through label sets. Restrict scrapes to observability namespaces and audit unexpected listeners.

Reliability: after fixing an incident, capture before-and-after internal metric snapshots in tickets to validate that queue_percentiles returned to expected steady state and that memlimiter_drop_rate returned to zero.

Continuous improvement: when Splunk releases new distribution images, revalidate default telemetry scrapes and update FIELDALIAS maps if metric names shift, recording upgrades in otel_pipeline_inventory notes fields.

Stakeholder communication: product owners care about trace completeness more than queue math; translate severity rows into probable customer impact using sampling rates and service maps already maintained by observability teams.

Tooling: optional ITSI modules can map collector_id entities to services when KPIs consume the same metrics indexes, but this UC remains valuable without premium modules because it is metrics-first.

Long-term architecture: evaluate dedicating separate gateway pools for logs versus traces versus metrics when noisy neighbors routinely collide; the same search will show which pipeline_type dominates saturation.

Post-incident review prompts ask whether persistent queues masked root cause duration, whether tokens rotated cleanly, and whether autoscaling policies require tighter reaction to exporter latency percentiles.

Vendor support bundles should include redacted YAML, internal metrics snapshots, and Splunk search results for the alert window to accelerate case resolution.

Community contrib processors such as probabilistic sampler or attributes extractors should be audited for CPU cost before promotion to gateway defaults.

Edge cases include ARM versus x86 instruction differences affecting parsing performance; note architecture in inventory when clusters are mixed.

IPv6-only environments must validate OTLP gRPC connectivity over dual-stack services; join failures may present as exporter retries without obvious DNS errors.

Services meshes may inject mTLS while collectors expect plaintext to sidecars; document TLS intent in inventory to avoid chasing Splunk when mesh policy is the true culprit.

Rate limiting at API gateways can cap requests per second independently of payload size; correlate gateway access logs with exporter failures when HTTP 429 appears.

Cold start bursts on Kubernetes after node drains can spike receiver_refused counters; pair with node lifecycle timelines before paging application teams.

Clock skew between collectors and backends distorts latency-based diagnostics; keep NTP discipline and record skew alerts separately.

Large attribute blobs from poorly behaved instrumentation can expand memory footprint; attribute size limits and defensive transforms belong in processor sections.

When using filelog receivers, aggressive poll intervals can starve exporter threads; tune poll intervals coherently with batch timeouts.

Windows event log receivers combined with heavy parsing can bottleneck single-threaded stages; migrate parsing to gateways when hosts show CPU saturation.

Mainframe proxy collectors forwarding z/OS metrics may batch differently; validate units before comparing to Linux gateway thresholds.

Cloud vendor FaaS collectors may have shorter lifetimes; ensure inventory marks ephemeral collector_id patterns so baselines do not misfire.

Finally, remember that human judgment still validates any automated severity; this search is a guide, not a substitute for context.
spl:
`comment("UC-13.3.15 OTel Collector pipeline backpressure multisearch internal metrics logs kube cmdb enrichment earliest=-2h@h")`
| multisearch
    [ | mstats latest(_value) AS mv WHERE index=otel_metrics metric_name IN ("otelcol_receiver_accepted_spans","otelcol_receiver_accepted_metric_points","otelcol_receiver_accepted_log_records","otelcol_receiver_refused_spans","otelcol_receiver_refused_metric_points","otelcol_receiver_refused_log_records") span=1m BY metric_name service_instance_id exporter receiver
      | eval collector_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(service_instance_id,"otel_unk"))))
      | eval pipeline_type=case(match(metric_name,"log_record"),"logs", match(metric_name,"metric_point"),"metrics", match(metric_name,"span"),"traces", true(),"mixed")
      | eval lane="mstats_rx"
      | fields _time collector_id pipeline_type metric_name mv exporter receiver lane ]
    [ | mstats latest(_value) AS mv WHERE index=otel_metrics metric_name IN ("otelcol_exporter_queue_size","otelcol_exporter_queue_capacity","otelcol_exporter_send_failed_spans","otelcol_exporter_send_failed_metric_points","otelcol_exporter_send_failed_log_records","otelcol_processor_dropped_spans","otelcol_processor_batches_sent") span=1m BY metric_name service_instance_id exporter
      | eval collector_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(service_instance_id,"otel_unk"))))
      | eval pipeline_type=case(match(metric_name,"log_record"),"logs", match(metric_name,"metric_point"),"metrics", match(metric_name,"span"),"traces", match(metric_name,"processor_batches"),"batch", true(),"mixed")
      | eval lane="mstats_ex"
      | fields _time collector_id pipeline_type metric_name mv exporter lane ]
    [ | mstats latest(_value) AS mv WHERE index=otel_metrics metric_name IN ("otelcol_processor_memorylimiter_drops","otelcol_processor_batch_batch_send_size","otelcol_processor_batch_timeout_trigger_send") span=1m BY metric_name service_instance_id
      | eval collector_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(service_instance_id,"otel_unk"))))
      | eval pipeline_type=case(match(metric_name,"memorylimiter"),"memory", true(),"batch")
      | eval lane="mstats_lim"
      | fields _time collector_id pipeline_type metric_name mv lane ]
    [ search (index=otel OR index=observability_logs OR index=containers) sourcetype=otel:collector earliest=-2h@h latest=now
      | eval rawl=lower(_raw)
      | where match(rawl,"queue is full|exporter failure|resource exhausted|backpressure|memory.?limit|retry|payload too large|send failed|refused")
      | eval collector_id=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(collector_id,service_instance_id,pod,host,"otel_log"))))
      | eval pod_name=trim(toString(coalesce(pod,pod_name,"")))
      | eval pipeline_type="mixed"
      | eval metric_name="log_line_backpressure"
      | eval mv=1.0
      | eval lane="otel_logs"
      | fields _time collector_id pipeline_type metric_name mv lane pod_name ]
    [ | mstats latest(_value) AS mv WHERE index=prometheus_metrics metric_name IN ("container_cpu_usage_seconds_total","container_memory_working_set_bytes") span=1m BY pod namespace
      | where match(lower(pod),"otel|splunk-otel|collector|opentelemetry")
      | eval collector_id=lower(trim(toString(pod)))
      | eval pod_name=collector_id
      | eval pipeline_type="kube"
      | eval metric_name=metric_name
      | eval lane="kube_pod"
      | fields _time collector_id pipeline_type metric_name mv lane pod_name ]
| eval pod_name=coalesce(pod_name,"unknown_pod")
| eval pipeline_type=coalesce(pipeline_type,"mixed")
| bucket _time span=1m
| eval v_acc=if(match(metric_name,"receiver_accepted"),mv,0)
| eval v_ref=if(match(metric_name,"receiver_refused"),mv,0)
| eval v_drop=if(match(metric_name,"processor_dropped"),mv,0)
| eval v_fail=if(match(metric_name,"send_failed"),mv,0)
| eval v_qs=if(match(metric_name,"exporter_queue_size"),mv,null())
| eval v_qc=if(match(metric_name,"exporter_queue_capacity"),mv,null())
| eval v_mem=if(match(metric_name,"memorylimiter_drops"),mv,0)
| eval v_batch=if(match(metric_name,"batch_batch_send_size"),mv,null())
| stats sum(v_acc) AS acc sum(v_ref) AS ref sum(v_drop) AS drop sum(v_fail) AS fail max(v_qs) AS qsz max(v_qc) AS qcap sum(v_mem) AS memdrop perc95(v_batch) AS batch_p95_ms values(lane) AS lanes first(pod_name) AS pod_name BY _time collector_id pipeline_type
| sort 0 collector_id pipeline_type - _time
| streamstats window=5 global=f current=t first(acc) AS acc0 last(acc) AS acc1 BY collector_id pipeline_type
| streamstats window=5 global=f current=t first(ref) AS ref0 last(ref) AS ref1 BY collector_id pipeline_type
| streamstats window=5 global=f current=t first(fail) AS fail0 last(fail) AS fail1 BY collector_id pipeline_type
| eval receiver_accepted_rate=round((acc1-acc0)/5,4)
| eval receiver_refused_rate=round((ref1-ref0)/5,4)
| eval processor_dropped_rate=round(drop,4)
| eval attempts=receiver_accepted_rate+receiver_refused_rate+0.0001
| eval exporter_failed_rate=round(100*(fail1-fail0)/attempts,3)
| eval queue_pct_full=if(qcap>0 AND isnotnull(qsz), round(100*qsz/qcap,2), 0)
| streamstats window=10 global=f min(queue_pct_full) AS qmin10 BY collector_id pipeline_type
| eval memlimiter_drop_rate=round(memdrop,4)
| eval sustained_hi_queue=if(qmin10>85,1,0)
| eventstats median(receiver_accepted_rate) AS env_med_rx BY collector_id
| eval severity=case(sustained_hi_queue==1 OR exporter_failed_rate>5 OR memlimiter_drop_rate>0,"critical", queue_pct_full>70 OR exporter_failed_rate>2 OR receiver_refused_rate>0.25,"high", queue_pct_full>50 OR receiver_refused_rate>0.05 OR processor_dropped_rate>0,"medium", true(),"low")
| eval recommended_action=case(severity=="critical","scale_gateway_or_agents_tune_batch_memory_limiter_validate_hec_otlp_tokens_persistent_queue_disk", severity=="high","widen_exporter_sending_queue_review_retry_backoff_and_downstream_ingest_capacity", severity=="medium","inspect_processor_order_k8sattributes_resource_limits_and_tls_for_exporters", true(),"monitor_baseline_revisit_thresholds_after_change_window")
| join type=left max=0 collector_id
    [| inputlookup otel_collector_inventory.csv
     | fields collector_id env owner_team role image_tag ]
| join type=left collector_id [search index=cmdb sourcetype=otel_pipeline_inventory | dedup collector_id]
| eval env=coalesce(env,"unknown_env")
| eval owner_team=coalesce(owner_team,"unowned")
| table _time collector_id env owner_team pod_name pipeline_type receiver_accepted_rate receiver_refused_rate processor_dropped_rate exporter_failed_rate queue_pct_full batch_p95_ms memlimiter_drop_rate severity recommended_action
cimSpl:
| tstats summariesonly=true latest(Performance.cpu_load_percent) AS cpu_load latest(Performance.mem_used_percent) AS mem_used FROM datamodel=Performance WHERE nodename=Performance host=* earliest=-24h@h latest=now BY Performance.host
| rename Performance.host AS metric_host
| join type=left max=0 metric_host
    [| inputlookup otel_collector_inventory.csv
     | eval metric_host=lower(trim(toString(collector_id)))
     | fields metric_host env owner_team role]
| where like(lower(metric_host),"%otel%") OR like(lower(metric_host),"%collector%")
| table metric_host cpu_load mem_used env owner_team role
dataModelAcceleration: Accelerate Performance summaries for hosts and pods mapped as Performance.host when collector CPU and memory pressure are modeled; use Inventory summaries or lookup-backed Inventory tags for collector fleet rows when available; retain thirty days for quarterly capacity attestations.
visualization: Severity-colored table by collector_id and pipeline_type with sparklines for queue_pct_full and exporter_failed_rate, drilldowns to internal metrics rows, otel:collector logs, kube pod series, and joined inventory columns; single-value tiles for sustained_hi_queue counts per env.

references.title: Splunk Docs — Splunk Distribution of OpenTelemetry Collector
OpenTelemetry Collector Contrib — memory limiter processor
OpenTelemetry Docs — SDK environment variables (retry and backoff concepts)
Splunk Docs — HTTP Event Collector examples and guidance

knownFalsePositives: Chaos load tests and fault injection drills that deliberately throttle exporters can saturate queues and raise critical severities without customer impact; annotate drills in change calendars before muting. Planned Splunk Cloud or Observability Cloud ingest tier upgrades may shift latency envelopes; freeze thresholds during vendor maintenance windows and compare against published status pages. Intentional gateway throttling during cost-governance events can elevate refused counters even when applications are healthy; require finance-approved runbook links before paging service owners. Development clusters with idle traffic may produce noisy low-rate refused spikes when collectors recycle connections; gate non-production envs with stricter filters. Canary deployments that sample traces aggressively can drop trace fractions that resemble processor drops until sampling policies stabilize; correlate with deployment metadata. Short bursts during certificate rotations may elevate exporter_failed_rate for minutes; overlap certificate validity windows to avoid false critical pages. Kubernetes node drains produce ephemeral CPU spikes on collector DaemonSets; pair with node cordon timelines before declaring incidents. Mis-labeled pods that match otel substrings but are not collectors can skew kube_pod lanes; tighten pod name filters when false positives recur. Stale otel_collector_inventory.csv rows that omit decommissioned collectors can mis-route ownership; refresh lookups weekly. Duplicate scraping of internal metrics from two Prometheus jobs can double counts until dedupe rules land; watch for sudden step changes after config edits.
hardware: Applies to Linux and Windows collector hosts, Kubernetes DaemonSet agents and gateway Deployments, Prometheus or OpenTelemetry scrapers feeding Splunk metrics indexes, and Splunk Enterprise or Splunk Cloud receiving HEC, OTLP, or Observability Cloud forwarded telemetry.
exclusions: Does not replace UC-13.3.2 Prometheus scrape and federation health analytics, UC-13.3.5 alert storm detection across paging vendors, per-application golden signal SLO dashboards, or secure software supply chain attestations. Does not mutate collector YAML, Kubernetes objects, or Splunk tokens automatically; it produces evidence and routing guidance for human-approved changes.

controlTest.positiveScenario: In a lab cluster, induce exporter backoff toward a paused Splunk HEC token while internal metrics show rising otelcol_exporter_queue_size and non-zero otelcol_exporter_send_failed_spans, confirm observability_uc_13_3_15_otel_pipeline_backpressure returns critical or high with non-null recommended_action within two five-minute windows.
controlTest.negativeScenario: After recovery when queues drain, send_failed counters return to zero, collector logs show clean exports, and pod metrics normalize, confirm severities return to low or medium baselines and the alert wrapper emits zero rows when filtering to critical and high only.

evidence: Saved search observability_uc_13_3_15_otel_pipeline_backpressure on a five-minute schedule; exported CSV joined to otel_collector_inventory.csv and otel_pipeline_inventory; dashboard drilldowns across internal metrics, collector logs, and pod series archived to a restricted observability evidence index.
